candidate_problems.text: How should 'trust' be formally modeled so the conjecture is not merely metaphorical? The core unresolved tension is whether trust is truly a non-excludable public-good stock, or instead a family of partially excludable, club-like, relational, and domain-specific assets. A new problem is to identify the right ontology and dynamics: what exactly accumulates, what depletes, at what level (dyadic, network, institutional, societal), and under what conditions local trust can coexist with global distrust or vice versa.

What mechanisms actually reverse low-trust equilibria once costly substitutes for trust—monitoring, bureaucracy, collateral, surveillance, in-group favoritism—have become adaptive and self-reinforcing? The open question is not just how trust erodes, but how path dependence is broken when the system structurally rewards distrust-management industries and practices. This is worth pursuing as a problem of transition dynamics, tipping points, and leverage: which institutional changes alter payoffs enough to regenerate trust rather than merely manage its absence.

How can the conjecture distinguish structural underprovision from genuine variation in norms, values, or moral commitments without collapsing one into the other? The unresolved tension is explanatory: if people respond to incentives, culture may itself be an evolved stock shaped by institutions; but if norms have causal autonomy, the 'trust as infrastructure' lens may be incomplete. A productive new problem is to determine when incentive redesign is sufficient, when cultural change is necessary, and how the two interact across timescales.
